models.ReviewEstimate: A human review session on an AI-generated estimate. Tracks per-feature, per-risk, and team adjustments with mandatory reasoning and real-time impact summary.
models.ReviewFeature: A feature under review with AI-original and human-adjusted values side by side.
models.FeatureAdjustment: The adjustment record for a single feature: status, original vs adjusted values, and reasoning.
models.ReviewRisk: A risk under review with original and potentially adjusted severity, impact, and mitigation.
models.TeamAdjustment: The team composition adjustment with original and adjusted roles and headcount.
models.ImpactSummary: Real-time summary of the cumulative impact of all human adjustments on the estimate.

review.overallConfidenceReasoning: After reviewing the full estimate, I'm raising confidence from 72% to 78%. The EHR integration risk is real but the team has handled this before on MedConnect. Telehealth scope is well-contained at 1:1 only. Main concern remains the compliance review timeline.

features.name: Patient Dashboard
features.description: Personalized landing page showing upcoming appointments, recent lab results, active prescriptions, and unread messages from care team.
assumptions: Dashboard widgets are pre-defined, not user-configurable.
Lab results are pulled from the EHR, not entered manually.

features.name: Appointment Scheduling
features.description: Book, reschedule, and cancel appointments with provider availability lookup and timezone handling.
assumptions: Provider availability comes from the EHR scheduling API.
No waitlist or auto-suggest functionality in v1.

adjustment.reasoning: EHR scheduling APIs are notoriously inconsistent across providers. On MedConnect we burned 30+ extra hours on timezone edge cases and availability sync lag. Bumping likely by 20h and widening the range to account for this.

features.name: Secure Messaging
features.description: HIPAA-compliant messaging between patients and care team members with attachment support and read receipts.
assumptions: Messages are stored encrypted at rest with AES-256.
Attachments are limited to images and PDFs under 10MB.
No real-time typing indicators — polling or push-based delivery only.

adjustment.reasoning: We've built HIPAA messaging three times now. Our internal encryption-at-rest module handles AES-256 out of the box. Downgrading complexity to medium and trimming hours — this is well-understood territory for us.

features.name: Telehealth Video Visits
features.description: Browser-based video consultations with waiting room, screen sharing, and session recording consent flow.
assumptions: Uses a third-party video SDK (Twilio or Daily.co).
Session recording is opt-in and stored externally.

adjustment.reasoning: Bumping to very_high complexity. The consent flow for session recording intersects with HIPAA in non-trivial ways — we need legal review of the UX copy, and the recording storage architecture needs its own spike. Also, screen sharing on Safari is still flaky with most SDKs. Adding 15h buffer.

features.name: Insurance Verification
features.description: Verify patient insurance eligibility in real-time via payer API integration, display coverage details, and flag discrepancies.
assumptions: Integration with a single eligibility clearinghouse (e.g., Availity).
Manual override available if API is unavailable.

features.name: Authentication & RBAC
features.description: Multi-factor authentication, role-based access control for patients, providers, and admin staff, session management with HIPAA-compliant timeouts.
assumptions: Uses an identity provider (Auth0 or Okta) — not built from scratch.

features.name: Lab Results Viewer
features.description: Display lab results from EHR with trend charts, reference ranges, and plain-language explanations powered by AI.
assumptions: Lab data follows HL7 FHIR format from the EHR.
AI explanations are generated at read-time, not pre-computed.

features.name: Prescription Management
features.description: View active and past prescriptions, request refills, and receive renewal reminders.
assumptions: Refill requests are sent to the provider — no e-prescribing in v1.
Prescription data pulled from EHR, read-only.

risks.title: EHR API instability and rate limits
risks.mitigation: Build a caching layer and circuit breaker pattern for EHR calls. Negotiate rate limit increases early. Budget 2 extra sprint days for API quirks.
risks.originalMitigation: Build a caching layer and circuit breaker pattern for EHR calls. Negotiate rate limit increases early. Budget 2 extra sprint days for API quirks.

risks.title: Telehealth scope creep into group calls and recording management
risks.mitigation: Lock v1 scope to 1:1 calls with opt-in recording. Defer group calls and recording management to v2. Get explicit client sign-off on v1 scope.
risks.originalMitigation: Lock v1 scope to 1:1 calls with opt-in recording. Defer group calls and recording management to v2.

adjustment.reasoning: Added explicit client sign-off requirement to the mitigation. We've seen scope creep happen mid-sprint when the client 'assumed' group calls were included. Written sign-off prevents this.

risks.title: Delayed EHR sandbox credentials from client
risks.mitigation: Start with mock EHR data and build an adapter layer. Escalate credential request in week 1 kickoff. Add contractual SLA for credential delivery.
risks.originalMitigation: Start with mock EHR data and build an adapter layer. Escalate credential request in week 1 kickoff.

adjustment.reasoning: Upgrading likelihood from medium to high. In 4 of our last 5 healthcare projects, EHR credentials arrived 2-4 weeks late. This is the norm, not the exception. Added contractual SLA to mitigation.

risks.title: HIPAA compliance review extends timeline by 2-4 weeks
risks.mitigation: Engage the compliance auditor in parallel with development, not sequentially. Budget a 2-week buffer.
risks.originalMitigation: Engage the compliance auditor in parallel with development, not sequentially. Budget a 2-week buffer.

risks.title: Healthcare domain expertise gap on the team
risks.mitigation: Assign a healthcare-experienced tech lead or bring in a domain consultant for the first 3 sprints.
risks.originalMitigation: Assign a healthcare-experienced tech lead or bring in a domain consultant for the first 3 sprints.

risks.title: Safari WebRTC compatibility issues for telehealth
risks.mitigation: Spike Safari compatibility in week 2. Have a fallback to phone-based audio if video fails. Test on iOS Safari specifically.

adjustment.reasoning: Adding new risk based on recent experience. Every telehealth project we've done has had Safari WebRTC issues — it's predictable enough to plan for explicitly.

originalRoles.reasoning: Owns architecture, EHR integration strategy, and HIPAA compliance coordination.
Core feature development across patient dashboard, scheduling, messaging, and prescriptions.
Telehealth UI, responsive design, and accessibility compliance.
HIPAA-focused test plans, integration test coverage for EHR flows, and security testing.
Healthcare UX patterns, accessibility standards, and patient-facing design that balances clarity with information density.

adjustedRoles.reasoning: Owns architecture, EHR integration strategy, and HIPAA compliance coordination.
Core feature development across patient dashboard, scheduling, messaging, and prescriptions.
Upgrading to senior — telehealth video and Safari compatibility require deep browser API expertise.
HIPAA-focused test plans, integration test coverage for EHR flows, and security testing.
Healthcare UX patterns, accessibility standards, and patient-facing design that balances clarity with information density.
HIPAA-compliant infrastructure setup, CI/CD, and EHR integration environment management.

teamAdjustment.reasoning: Adding a DevOps engineer to handle the HIPAA-compliant infrastructure from day one. Also upgrading the frontend engineer to senior — the telehealth and Safari compatibility work demands someone who's dealt with WebRTC quirks before. This brings the team to 7.
